summary: Gruber reports on his experience with the Apple Studio Display after a month of daily use. He's ordering the nano-texture version with height-adjustable stand, finding the anti-reflective finish worth the premium despite slightly less vibrant colors. The main issue he encountered was completely garbled audio that could only be fixed by physically unplugging the display from the wall, since there's no power button or software restart option. He argues that a display powered by an embedded iOS computer should be at least as reliable as a refrigerator, and that if Apple designed it without a power button, it should never need power-cycling. He also notes the continued silence from Apple on fixing the Studio Display's poor camera quality.
key_points: The nano-texture finish eliminates reflections but looks slightly less vibrant than the glossy version — a tradeoff Gruber prefers and would pay extra for on a MacBook Pro too
Garbled audio required physically unplugging the display to fix, since there's no power button or software restart command
The Studio Display is essentially a self-contained iOS computer under the hood, but that architectural choice creates reliability expectations it isn't meeting
Apple has been silent on fixing the Studio Display's poor camera quality, with no firmware updates since launch
money_quotes: But "pull the power cord out of the wall" is not exactly an intuitive solution to glitchy audio.
If you're going to design a display without a power button, it ought never need to be power-cycled.
I expect a display from the world's most-renowned computer company to be at least as reliable a computer as my refrigerator.
It is fascinating that the Studio Display is, under the hood, a self-contained iOS computer, but the overwhelming majority of Studio Display owners will never know that, nor should they.
A monitor is the sort of thing you expect to plug in and never need to unplug — certainly not just to get sound working.
I'm resigned to accepting that the Studio Display just has a crappy camera.
key_insight: If Apple is going to embed a full iOS computer inside a display and omit a power button, it has an obligation to make it as reliable as any other appliance — and it's not there yet.
